semantic_description: A mini cable worked over 2 stitches without a cable needle. The right stitch crosses over the left, both knit. Creates a rightward-traveling knit stitch on a knit ground. The simplest cable unit — only 2 stitches wide. Can be worked by skipping the first stitch, knitting the second, then knitting the first, or by a quick reorientation on the needle.

written_forms: skip the first stitch, knit the second stitch, then knit the skipped stitch, slide both off
knit 2 stitches out of order, right over left
right twist: k2nd st on needle, then k1st st, sl both off together
ambiguity_notes: 'RT' (right twist) is the most common US abbreviation (used by Purl Soho, Brooklyn Tweed, Interweave) but ALSO collides with '1/1 RC' cable notation in Stitchmastery and some publishers who use 'RT' for a needle-cable version. The distinction is mechanical: twist_right is typically worked without a cable needle (knit into 2nd stitch, then 1st, both slipped off). '1/1 RC' implies using a cable needle or a structural cross. When both appear in the same legend, treat as distinct. 'T2R' is the Rowan/Debbie Bliss convention for this same no-needle twist. 'C2R' is used by some US publishers.

'RT' and 'LT' are used both for 2-stitch no-cable-needle twists and for small cables. Some UK patterns use 'T2B'/'T2F' for traveling stitches on purl ground, which is a different operation (twist purl variants).
Symbols or glossaries labeled '1/1 RC' or '2-st Right Cable' are modeled separately as the explicit cable family worked with a cable needle.
Ratio-prefixed forms such as '1/1 RT', '2/1 RT', or '2/2 RT' usually refer to twisted cable stitches worked with a cable needle, not this no-cable-needle traveling stitch.

rs: Skip the first stitch on the left needle
Insert right needle into the second stitch and knit it, leaving both stitches on left needle
Slide both stitches off the left needle

semantic_description: A mini cable worked over 2 stitches without a cable needle. The left stitch crosses over the right, both knit. Creates a leftward-traveling knit stitch on a knit ground. The mirror of twist right.

written_forms: slip first stitch behind, knit second stitch through back loop, then knit first stitch, slide both off
knit 2 stitches out of order, left over right
ambiguity_notes: Same collision as twist_right: 'LT' is the most common US abbreviation but collides with '1/1 LC' in some systems. 'T2L' is Rowan/Debbie Bliss convention.
Symbols or glossaries labeled '1/1 LC' or '2-st Left Cable' are modeled separately as the explicit cable family worked with a cable needle.
Ratio-prefixed forms such as '1/1 LT', '2/1 LT', or '2/2 LT' usually refer to twisted cable stitches worked with a cable needle, not this no-cable-needle traveling stitch.

rs: Reach behind the first stitch on left needle
Knit the second stitch through the back loop (or from behind)
Then knit the first stitch normally
Slide both off the left needle

semantic_description: A 2-stitch traveling stitch where a knit stitch crosses rightward over a purl stitch. The right stitch is knit and the left (background) stitch is purled. Creates a knit stitch that travels diagonally to the right across a purl ground. Essential for Aran and Celtic cable patterns where cables travel across reverse stockinette backgrounds.

written_forms: slip 1 to cable needle and hold in back, knit 1, purl 1 from cable needle
right purl cross: 1 knit stitch travels right over 1 purl stitch
ambiguity_notes: 'T2B' (twist 2 back) is used by Rowan and Debbie Bliss specifically to mean: slip 1 to back, knit 1, purl slipped stitch — producing a right-leaning purl twist. Some patterns use 'RPT' (right purl twist). Rowan uses 'T2B' where the purl stitch goes behind. Collision: 'T2B' is also used by some publishers for a plain 2-stitch right cable — always verify with legend.
'T2B' in some publishers means 'twist 2 back' (right purl cross), in others it means 'twist 2' without purl (same as TwR). Context and the pattern's glossary are essential.
'RPT' and 'T2Bp' are also used for the same fabric result in some stitch glossaries, especially when the stitch is worked without a cable needle.
Symbols or glossaries labeled '1/1 RPC' or '2-st Right Purl Cable' are modeled separately as the explicit cable family notation.
Ratio-prefixed purl-twist forms such as '1/1 RPT' or '2/1 RPT' usually mean twisted cable crossings where the knit stitches are worked through the back loop.

rs: Slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at back of work

semantic_description: A 2-stitch traveling stitch where a knit stitch crosses leftward over a purl stitch. The left stitch is knit and the right (background) stitch is purled. Creates a knit stitch that travels diagonally to the left across a purl ground. The mirror of twist right purl.

written_forms: slip 1 to cable needle and hold in front, purl 1, knit 1 from cable needle
left purl cross: 1 knit stitch travels left over 1 purl stitch
ambiguity_notes: 'T2F' (twist 2 front) is the Rowan/Debbie Bliss convention for left purl twist. 'LPT' is used by some US publishers. Same collision risk as T2B: can mean a plain cable cross in some patterns.
Symbols or glossaries labeled '1/1 LPC' or '2-st Left Purl Cable' are modeled separately as the explicit cable family notation.
Ratio-prefixed purl-twist forms such as '1/1 LPT' or '2/1 LPT' usually mean twisted cable crossings where the knit stitches are worked through the back loop.

rs: Slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at front of work

semantic_description: A 2-stitch cable where the right stitch crosses over the left stitch. Both stitches are knit. This is the explicit 1/1 right-cross cable family used by chart legends and symbol sets such as '1/1 RC', '1/1 Right Cable', or '2-st Right Cable'. It usually implies a cable-style crossing worked with an auxiliary needle, even though the finished fabric can resemble a no-cable-needle right twist.

written_forms: slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at back, knit 1 from left needle, knit 1 from cable needle
1/1 right cross cable: sl1 to CN hold back, k1, k1 from CN
ambiguity_notes: '1/1 RC' is the Stitchmastery standard. 'C2B' (cable 2 back) is used by many UK publishers (Rowan, Debbie Bliss) and means hold 1 st to back, k1, k1. 'RT' is used by some publishers for this same operation when done with a cable needle — collides with twist_right (no cable needle method). Context and legend are definitive.

This family is often visually similar to a no-cable-needle right twist, but symbol sets that explicitly label the operation as '1/1 RC' or '2-st Right Cable' are naming the cable-family notation, not the shorthand traveling-stitch instruction.

rs: Slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at the BACK of work

semantic_description: A 2-stitch cable where the left stitch crosses over the right stitch. Both stitches are knit. This is the explicit 1/1 left-cross cable family used by chart legends and symbol sets such as '1/1 LC', '1/1 Left Cable', or '2-st Left Cable'. It usually implies a cable-style crossing worked with an auxiliary needle, even though the finished fabric can resemble a no-cable-needle left twist.

written_forms: slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at front, knit 1 from left needle, knit 1 from cable needle
1/1 left cross cable: sl1 to CN hold front, k1, k1 from CN
ambiguity_notes: '1/1 LC' is the Stitchmastery standard. 'C2F' (cable 2 front) is the UK publisher equivalent. 'LT' collides with twist_left — same ambiguity as 1/1 RC vs RT.

This family is often visually similar to a no-cable-needle left twist, but symbol sets that explicitly label the operation as '1/1 LC' or '2-st Left Cable' are naming the cable-family notation, not the shorthand traveling-stitch instruction.

rs: Slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at the FRONT of work

semantic_description: A 2-stitch purl-cross cable where a knit stitch travels rightward over a purl stitch. This is the explicit 1/1 right purl cable family used by chart legends and symbol sets such as '1/1 RPC', '1/1 Right Purl Cable', or '2-st Right Purl Cable'.

written_forms: slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at back, knit 1 from left needle, purl 1 from cable needle
1/1 right purl cross cable: sl1 to CN hold back, k1, p1 from CN
ambiguity_notes: '1/1 RPC' is the Stitchmastery standard. 'C2BP' (cable 2 back purl) and 'T2B' both appear in UK patterns. 'T2B' collision with twist_right_purl: some publishers use T2B for purl-involving cable, some for purl-involving no-needle twist — always check legend.

rs: Slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at the BACK of work

semantic_description: A 2-stitch purl-cross cable where a knit stitch travels leftward over a purl stitch. This is the explicit 1/1 left purl cable family used by chart legends and symbol sets such as '1/1 LPC', '1/1 Left Purl Cable', or '2-st Left Purl Cable'.

written_forms: slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at front, purl 1 from left needle, knit 1 from cable needle
1/1 left purl cross cable: sl1 to CN hold front, p1, k1 from CN
ambiguity_notes: '1/1 LPC' is the Stitchmastery standard. 'C2FP' and 'T2F' appear in UK patterns. Same T2F collision as twist_left_purl.

rs: Slip 1 stitch to cable needle and hold at the FRONT of work

semantic_description: A 4-stitch cable where the right 2 stitches cross over the left 2 stitches. All 4 stitches are knit. The cable twists to the right when viewed from the RS. One of the most common cable widths — wide enough to be clearly visible, narrow enough to be worked frequently. Hold 2 stitches at the back, knit 2, knit 2 from holder.

written_forms: slip 2 stitches to cable needle and hold at back, knit 2 from left needle, knit 2 from cable needle
cable 4 back: sl2 to CN hold back, k2, k2 from CN
ambiguity_notes: 'C4B' (cable 4 back) is the dominant UK/Rowan/Debbie Bliss form. '2/2 RC' is the Stitchmastery form. Both are extremely common. No genuine ambiguity between them — they map to the same operation. '4-st RC' is found in some US patterns.

C4B means 'cable 4, hold BACK' which produces a RIGHT cross. C4F means 'cable 4, hold FRONT' which produces a LEFT cross. The back/front refers to where the cable needle is held, not the visual direction. This is the #1 cable confusion point.
C4R means 'cable 4, RIGHT cross' — same operation, more intuitive naming.

rs: Slip the next 2 stitches onto cable needle and hold at the BACK of work
Knit the next 2 stitches from left needle
Knit the 2 stitches from cable needle

semantic_description: A 4-stitch cable where the left 2 stitches cross over the right 2 stitches. All 4 stitches are knit. The cable twists to the left when viewed from the RS. Hold 2 stitches at the front, knit 2, knit 2 from holder. The mirror of the 2/2 right cross.

written_forms: slip 2 stitches to cable needle and hold at front, knit 2 from left needle, knit 2 from cable needle
cable 4 front: sl2 to CN hold front, k2, k2 from CN
ambiguity_notes: 'C4F' (cable 4 front) is the dominant UK form. '2/2 LC' is Stitchmastery.

rs: Slip the next 2 stitches onto cable needle and hold at the FRONT of work
Knit the next 2 stitches from left needle
Knit the 2 stitches from cable needle

semantic_description: A 4-stitch cable cross where 2 knit stitches travel rightward over 2 purl stitches. The purl stitches form the background, and the knit stitches cross in front moving right. Used in Aran patterns where cables travel across a purl (reverse stockinette) ground. Hold 2 purl stitches at front, knit 2, purl 2 from holder.

written_forms: slip 2 to cable needle and hold at back, knit 2, purl 2 from cable needle
right purl cross: sl2 to CN hold back, k2, p2 from CN
ambiguity_notes: 'C4BP' (cable 4 back purl) and 'T4B' (twist 4 back) are both used by UK publishers. Rowan uses T4B. Some patterns use 'C4BP'. All mean: hold 2 sts to back, k2, p2.

rs: Slip 2 stitches to cable needle, hold at BACK

semantic_description: A 4-stitch cable cross where 2 knit stitches travel leftward over 2 purl stitches. The mirror of the 2/2 right purl cross. Hold 2 knit stitches at front, purl 2, knit 2 from holder.

written_forms: slip 2 to cable needle and hold at front, purl 2, knit 2 from cable needle
left purl cross: sl2 to CN hold front, p2, k2 from CN
ambiguity_notes: 'C4FP' and 'T4F' are UK publisher equivalents. Rowan uses T4F.

rs: Slip 2 stitches to cable needle, hold at FRONT

semantic_description: A 6-stitch cable where the right 3 stitches cross over the left 3 stitches. All 6 stitches are knit. A bold, chunky cable. Hold 3 at back, knit 3, knit 3 from holder.

written_forms: slip 3 to CN hold back, k3, k3 from CN
ambiguity_notes: 'C6B' is the dominant UK form. '3/3 RC' is Stitchmastery.

rs: Slip 3 to cable needle, hold at back

semantic_description: A 6-stitch cable where the left 3 stitches cross over the right 3. All knit. Hold 3 at front, knit 3, knit 3 from holder.

written_forms: slip 3 to CN hold front, k3, k3 from CN

rs: Slip 3 to cable needle, hold at front

semantic_description: A large 8-stitch cable where the right 4 stitches cross over the left 4. Creates a dramatic, bold cable rope. Hold 4 at back, knit 4, knit 4 from holder.

written_forms: slip 4 to CN hold back, k4, k4 from CN

rs: Slip 4 to cable needle, hold at back

semantic_description: A large 8-stitch cable where the left 4 stitches cross over the right 4. Hold 4 at front, knit 4, knit 4 from holder.

written_forms: slip 4 to CN hold front, k4, k4 from CN

rs: Slip 4 to cable needle, hold at front

semantic_description: An asymmetric 3-stitch cable where 2 stitches cross over 1 stitch to the right. All knit. Creates a smaller cable that's useful for diagonal lines and branching cable patterns. Hold 1 at back, knit 2, knit 1 from holder.

written_forms: slip 1 to CN hold back, k2, k1 from CN
ambiguity_notes: 'C3B' is the UK publisher form for a 3-stitch right cable (2 over 1). Some patterns use 'C3R'. This is an asymmetric cable.

rs: Slip 1 to cable needle, hold at back

semantic_description: An asymmetric 3-stitch cable where 2 stitches cross over 1 stitch to the left. All knit. Hold 2 at front, knit 1, knit 2 from holder.

written_forms: slip 2 to CN hold front, k1, k2 from CN

rs: Slip 2 to cable needle, hold at front

semantic_description: A complex 7-stitch three-element right purl cross. Three knit stitches cross rightward over a single center purl stitch and then over three more knit stitches. Creates a dramatic wide cable with a center purl channel. Used in complex Aran panels.

alternate_names: wide right purl cable with center purl
written_forms: slip 4 to CN hold back, k3, slip center st from CN back to left needle and purl it, k3 from CN
ambiguity_notes: No widely-used publisher shorthand for this specific 3-group cable. '3/1/3 RPC' is the Stitchmastery-style notation and the most unambiguous. Some patterns write it out in full.

rs: Slip 4 stitches to cable needle and hold at back
Slip the center stitch from cable needle back to left needle and purl it
Knit 3 remaining stitches from cable needle

semantic_description: The mirror of the 3/1/3 right purl cross. Three knit stitches cross leftward over a center purl stitch and three more knit stitches.

written_forms: slip 3 to CN hold front, slip next st to 2nd CN hold back, k3, p1 from back CN, k3 from front CN

rs: Slip 3 stitches to first cable needle, hold at front
Slip 1 center stitch to second cable needle, hold at back

semantic_description: A 5-stitch three-element right purl cross. Two knit stitches cross rightward over a single center purl stitch and then over two more knit stitches. It is the 5-stitch counterpart to the 3/1/3 purl-cross cable.

written_forms: slip 3 to CN hold back, k2, slip center st from CN to left needle and purl it, k2 from CN

rs: Slip 3 stitches to cable needle, hold at back
Slip center stitch from CN back to left needle, purl it

semantic_description: The mirror of the 2/1/2 right purl cross. Two knit stitches cross leftward over a center purl stitch and two more knit stitches.

written_forms: slip 2 to CN hold front, slip 1 to 2nd CN hold back, k2, p1 from back CN, k2 from front CN

rs: Slip 2 to first CN, hold front
Slip 1 to second CN, hold back
